coredata.dc:title: Epimedium brevicornu maxim. -derived green carbon quantum dots as chemiluminescence probes for highly sensitive detection of Sulfacetamide and Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yue
Xiao, Yujie
Yang, Xian
Zhang, Xue
coredata.dc:description: This study presented the synthesis of phosphorus (P) and chlorine (Cl) co-doped carbon quantum dots (P/Cl-CQDs) derived from Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. and established a simple and effective chemiluminescence (CL) system for detecting Sulfacetamide (STM) and Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt (STX). Employing traditional Chinese medicine Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. as a green precursor material, (NH4)2HPO4 and NH4Cl were utilized as dopants for P and Cl, respectively. The P/Cl-CQDs was synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method exhibited excellent CL properties, effectively catalyzing the luminol system and markedly amplifying its CL intensity. Both STM and STX were observed to inhibit the CL intensity of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system. Based on this, a novel CL method combining flow injection technique was established for the detection of STM and STX. Under optimal conditions, the method demonstrated the capability to detect STM in the range of 1–400 × 10−6 mol/L, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 × 10−6 mol/L and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.29 %, and STX within the range of 5–280 × 10−6 mol/L, with a LOD of 0.16 × 10−6 mol/L and an RSD of 1.23 %. This method successfully determined the content of STM and STX in pork, with spike recoveries ranging from 100.0 % to 103.8 % for STM and from 98.0 % to 102.3 % for STX.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Epimedium brevicornu Maxim.
Carbon Quantum Dots
P and Cl Co-doped
Sulfacetamide
Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt

CL intensity and stability of P/Cl-CQDs
Preparation conditions and concentration optimization of P/Cl-CQDs
The linear response range and limit of detection
Real sample analysis of TFA and STZ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of STM and STX detection.
TEM images of P/Cl-CQDs at different magnifications, insets of (a) and (c) are histograms of size distribution and the HR-TEM image of P/Cl-CQDs.

(a) Full XPS spectra of P/Cl-CQDs, (b-e) XPS high-resolution spectra of Cl 2p, O 1 s, O 1 s and P 2p of P/Cl-CQDs, (f) Full XPS spectra of CQDs, (g-h) XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s of CQDs.

(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD spectra and (c) Raman spectra of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs, (d) UV–visible absorption spectra of P/Cl-CQDs, the inset of (d) shows the photographs of P/Cl-CQDs solutions under visible light (left), 254 nm UV light (middle)and 365 nm UV light (right).
(a) Standard curve of STM, (b) Standard curve of STX, (c) CL kinetic curve.
Effect of common interfering substances on the determination of STM and STX.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries obtained for the STM and STX in pork samples.
Comparison of STM and STX determinations using the proposed and reference methods.

head.ce:title: Epimedium brevicornu maxim. -derived green carbon quantum dots as chemiluminescence probes for highly sensitive detection of Sulfacetamide and Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Active Substance Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, College of Life of Science, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Active Substance Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Active Substance Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, College of Life of Science, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Chongqing 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presented the synthesis of phosphorus (P) and chlorine (Cl) co-doped carbon quantum dots (P/Cl-CQDs) derived from Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. and established a simple and effective chemiluminescence (CL) system for detecting Sulfacetamide (STM) and Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt (STX). Employing traditional Chinese medicine Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. as a green precursor material, (NH4)2HPO4 and NH4Cl were utilized as dopants for P and Cl, respectively. The P/Cl-CQDs was synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method exhibited excellent CL properties, effectively catalyzing the luminol system and markedly amplifying its CL intensity. Both STM and STX were observed to inhibit the CL intensity of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system. Based on this, a novel CL method combining flow injection technique was established for the detection of STM and STX. Under optimal conditions, the method demonstrated the capability to detect STM in the range of 1–400 × 10−6 mol/L, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 × 10−6 mol/L and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.29 %, and STX within the range of 5–280 × 10−6 mol/L, with a LOD of 0.16 × 10−6 mol/L and an RSD of 1.23 %. This method successfully determined the content of STM and STX in pork, with spike recoveries ranging from 100.0 % to 103.8 % for STM and from 98.0 % to 102.3 % for STX.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfacetamide (STM) and Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt (STX) are widely used sulfonamide medications. STM exhibits local antibacterial efficacy on the skin and eyes, commonly utilized to treat diseases such as tinea versicolor, rosacea, trachoma, and conjunctivitis [1]. Conversely, STX finds frequent application in the treatment and prevention of animal diseases, enhancing their growth performance [2,3]. While STX and STM are characterized by low toxicity, excessive usage may precipitate adverse reactions including allergic responses and digestive system disorders. Moreover, STX and STM may persist in the environment, undergoing bioaccumulation processes that augment their toxicity, thereby posing potential threats to aquatic and terrestrial organisms. Therefore, detecting STX and STM is crucial. Currently, techniques for STX and STM detection include spectrophotometry [4], voltammetry [5], chromatography [6,7], fluorescence immunoassay [8], and pressure-assisted liquid-phase microextraction [9]. While these methodologies are capable of STX and STM detection, they are afflicted by drawbacks including high cost, complexity, and time consumption. Therefore, developing a low-cost, simple, and fast method for detecting STX and STM is particularly important. Among various analytical methods, chemiluminescence has gradually become a potential approach for detecting STX and STM due to its simplicity, efficiency, and accuracy.

Chemiluminescence (CL) typically requires the involvement of catalysts, and nanomaterials serve as excellent choices. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterial widely used in areas including bioimaging, optical devices, and catalysis. CQDs offer advantages including tunable luminescent properties, excellent stability, simple synthesis, easy functionalization, cost-effectiveness, and low toxicity [10,11]. To enhance the applicability of CQDs in various fields, improvements can be made to their functionalities. Modulating the carbon skeleton matrix and chemical configuration of CQDs via heteroatom doping represents an efficacious approach for enhancing their optical and electronic characteristics. In this regard, dual-element co-doped CQDs exhibit unique electronic properties and have broad prospects in the field of photocatalysis [12,13]. Research has demonstrated that phosphorus (P) atom doping can preserve the fundamental traits of CQDs while proficiently modulating their physicochemical and photochemical attributes [14]. Additionally, halogen doping, exemplified by Cl, enables further improve the optical and physicochemical properties of CQDs, expanding their application in fluorescence sensors, biomedicine, photocatalysis, anti-counterfeiting encryption, and light-emitting diodes [15].

The utilization of biomass precursors for synthesizing CQDs offers advantages such as low cost, high efficiency, non-toxicity, and environmental friendliness, gradually establishing itself as the preferred method for CQDs synthesis. Epimedium brevicornu Maxim., a perennial herbaceous plant whose leaves are used in medicine, has a long history of medicinal use and widespread applications. It is known for its effects in tonifying kidney yang, strengthening tendons and bones, and dispelling wind and dampness [16]. Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. contains various active components including flavonoids, phenolic acids, amino acids, nucleosides, and alkaloids [17]. These components not only exhibit excellent pharmacological effects but can also undergo chemical reactions, making it a natural material for preparing CQDs.

In this study, Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. was utilized as a green precursor and employed dual-element doping of P and Cl. P/Cl-CQDs was synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method, and their morphology, elemental composition, surface functional groups, crystalline properties, and structure were characterized using various techniques including TEM, XPS, FTIR, XRD, and Raman. The prepared P/Cl-CQDs exhibited uniform particle size distribution, stable properties, good water solubility, abundant functional groups, and excellent CL properties. P/Cl-CQDs could catalyze the luminol system and significantly enhance its CL intensity, thus providing a novel method for the simultaneous detection of STM and STX through the formation of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) CL system. The stability and reliability of this method were verified through interference testing and recovery rate determination.

Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. specimens were collected from the Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. plantation located in Yongxin Town, Qijiang District, Chongqing. Sulfacetamide, Sulfadimethoxine Sodium Salt, and luminol were procured from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (NH4)2HPO4, NH4Cl, NaOH, and ascorbic acid were sourced from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. K3Fe(CN)6, Nitrotetrazolium Blue chloride, L-tryptophan, and thiourea were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Pork samples were acquired from the New Century Supermarket located in Chongqing University City.

1 g Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. powder was combined with 50 mL of distilled water and subjected to sonication for 45 min to ensure thorough mixing. The resulting solution was then sealed in a reaction vessel and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h, yielding CQDs, labeled as such. Additionally, 0.1 g of (NH4)2HPO4 (P source), 0.1 g of NH4Cl (Cl source), and a mixture of 0.1 g of (NH4)2HPO4 and 0.1 g of NH4Cl (both P and Cl sources) were separately added to the sonicated Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. solution. Other procedures remained unaltered, resulting in CQDs labelled as P-CQDs, Cl-CQDs, and P/Cl-CQDs, respectively. After the reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to eliminate insoluble impurities. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h using a 500–1000 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane to obtain four types of CQDs solutions. The process of CQDs preparation is illustrated in Fig. S1.

The morphology and lattice structure of P/Cl-CQDs were obtained using the Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, CZ). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USTM and STX). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded using the Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USTM and STX). X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) was obtained using the D8 ADVANCE X X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, GER). Raman spectroscopy was acquired using the Renishaw in Via Raman spectrometer (UK). The cathodoluminescence (CL) signal was recorded using the IFFM-D flow injection CL instrument (Xi'an Ruimai Electronic Technology Co., Ltd). UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis) was recorded using the TU-1810DSPC UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL detection for STM and STX
ce:section.ce:para: The pump speed of the flow injection CL (FI-CL) analyzer was set to 30 (r/min), while the high voltage applied to the photomultiplier tube was maintained at 600 V. A new CL system composed of P/Cl-CQDs, NaOH, luminol, and K3Fe(CN)6 was tested using the STM and STX detectors. Optimal flow conditions for detecting STM and STX were determined through optimization of the detection flow path, as shown in Fig. 1 . The experiment involved introducing solutions of NaOH, luminol, and K3Fe(CN)6 into the flow path through the main pump, while the solution of P/Cl-CQDs was introduced under pressure into the flow path through the auxiliary pump. The solutions from each system converged in a mixer before entering the detector, and the resulting CL signal could be monitored in real-time using a computer. For quantitative determination of STM or STX, the CL signal in the absence of STM or STX was denoted as I, and the CL signal in the presence of STM or STX was denoted as I0 . The decrease in CL intensity (ΔI = I − I0 ) was utilized for the quantification of STM or STX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL intensity and stability of P/Cl-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained four types of CQDs were individually added into the luminol system, and their CL signals were detected. The results in Fig. S2(a) indicated that the CL signal of P/Cl-CQDs was the strongest. This could be attributed to the effective increase in the number of functional groups on the surface of CQDs through co-doping of P and Cl. This enhancement allowed for better surface modification of the CQDs. Although doping with either P or Cl alone increased the CL signal of CQDs to some extent, the effect was inferior. Therefore, P/Cl-CQDs was selected for further experiments.

It was found that the CL intensity of the luminol system was significantly increased in the presence of P/Cl-CQDs, as shown in Fig. S2(b). In contrast, the CL intensity of the luminol system was relatively low when P/Cl-CQDs were absent under the same detection conditions. The addition of P/Cl-CQDs resulted in the formation of a new luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, which significant enhanced the CL intensity of the luminol system. The enhancement could be attributed to the presence of various functional groups such as –NH2, C = O, and –COOH on P/Cl-CQDs, which could enhance the catalytic ability of CQDs, accelerate the oxidation–reduction reaction, and thus enhancing the CL intensity.

The stability of P/Cl-CQDs was examined by recording the CL intensity of P/Cl-CQDs under prolonged storage, different pH values, and different temperatures. The results are shown in Fig.S3 (a) CDs stored in the refrigerator for more than 90 days still significantly enhanced the CL intensity of the luminol system. The sensitizing effect of P/Cl-CQDs on the luminol system was investigated in the pH range of 2–13, and the results are shown in Fig. S3 (b), which shows that the P/Cl-CQDs can still increase the CL intensity of the luminol system significantly under alkaline conditions, but the increase in the CL intensity of the luminol system by P/Cl-CQDs under acidic conditions was insignificant compared to P/Cl-CQDs that under alkaline conditions, which is probably due to the alkaline environment in which the luminol system is reacted. These results indicate that P/Cl-CQDs are highly stable and can be utilized in some extreme environments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) directly reflect the microstructure and size of CQDs. Analysis of P/Cl-CQDs was conducted utilizing a 300 kV TEM instrument. The TEM images of P/Cl-CQDs at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 2 . The prepared P/Cl-CQDs are observed to be spherical, uniformly distributed, and well-dispersed in Fig. 2(a) and 2(b). Analysis of approximately 500P/Cl-CQDs particles yielded a size distribution histogram (inset in Fig. 2a). According to the size distribution histogram, the particle size distribution of P/Cl-CQDs is narrow (ranging from 0.6 to 3.4 nm), with an average size of 1.82 nm. Fig. 2(c) presents the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of P/Cl-CQDs, indicating that P/Cl-CQDs possess a crystalline structure. The spacing between lattice fringes is measured to be 0.20 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of the (100) plane in graphite materials [18].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze the chemical composition and surface functional groups of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs. Fig. 3 (a) and 3(f) show the full XPS spectra of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs, respectively. In both spectra, two characteristic peaks at 531.9 eV and 285.3 eV were observed, corresponding to O 1 s and C 1 s [19], respectively. Carbon (C) constitutes the primary element in the structure of both P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs, while oxygen (O) is associated with oxygen-containing functional groups present on their surfaces. P/Cl-CQDs exhibit two peaks at 199.0 eV and 133.3 eV, corresponding to Cl 2p and P 2p, respectively [20], indicating the successful introduction of P and Cl into P/Cl-CQDs.

High-resolution XPS spectra was employed for further elucidation of their specific chemical structures and potential surface states. In Fig. 3b, the Cl 2p spectrum of P/Cl-CQDs reveals four discernible peaks at 197.6 eV, 199.1 eV, 200.0 eV, and 201.7 eV, corresponding to COOCl, CCl, OCCl, and ClCO, respectively [21–23]. Fig. 3c illustrates the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of P/Cl-CQDs, where peaks are discernible at 284.7 eV (CC/CC), 286.0 eV (CO), 287.3 eV (CCl), and 532.3 eV (CO) [20,24,25]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of P/Cl-CQDs (Fig. 3d) reveals peaks at 531.2 eV (CO) and 532.5 eV (CO) [26]. The P 2p spectrum of P/Cl-CQDs (Fig. 3e) exhibits peaks at 133.1 eV (PO) and 134.0 eV (PO) [26,27]. In comparison, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 3g) displays peaks at 284.9 eV (CC/CC) and 287.5 eV (CO) [28,29]. In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 3h), the peak at 531.4 eV is attributed to CO, while the peak at 532.5 eV corresponds to CO [25,26].

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis serves as a valuable tool for qualitatively describing the chemical structure of carbon materials [30]. Fig. 4 (a) displayed the FTIR spectra of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs. Both P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs exhibit peaks corresponding to NH around 3277 cm−1 [31], peaks corresponding to CH around 2932 cm−1 [20], and peaks corresponding to CO around 1024 cm−1 [32]. The peak of P/Cl-CQDs at 1636 cm−1 originates from CO/CC [33]. Peaks observed at 1368 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 in P/Cl-CQDs correspond to PO and POC, respectively [34], indicating successful doping of phosphorus (P) into P/Cl-CQDs. The peak observed at 767 cm−1 in P/Cl-CQDs arises from the vibration of CCl [27,31], indicating successful doping of chlorine (Cl) into P/Cl-CQDs. In the case of CQDs, the peak at 1589 cm−1 originates from CO/CC [35], while the peak at 1404 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN [31].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of both P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs were recorded to ascertain details regarding the crystal phase and composition of the synthesized samples. Fig. 4(b) illustrates the XRD measurement results of both P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs, revealing the presence of two prominent characteristic peaks in each case. A broad and intense diffraction peak centered at 2θ=19.8° corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon, indicating the amorphous nature of the materials [28]. Additionally, a weaker peak centered at 2θ=42° corresponds to the (100) plane of carbon [36]. These observations collectively indicate that both the synthesized P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs exhibit amorphous nanostructures.

Raman spectroscopy offers invaluable insights into the structural characteristics of both P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs. Fig. 4(c) presents the Raman spectra of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs. Peaks around 1343 cm−1 and 1353 cm−1 correspond to the D band of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs, respectively, indicating disordered sp3 carbon structures. Peaks around 1546 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 correspond to the crystalline G band of P/Cl-CQDs and CQDs, respectively, representing graphitic sp2 carbon structures. The ratio of the intensity of the D band to that of the G band (ID/IG) can be used to quantify the degree of graphitization [37]. In P/Cl-CQDs, the intensity of the G band is higher than that of the D band, indicating that s sp2 structures dominate in P/Cl-CQDs. The ID/IG ratio of P/Cl-CQDs is 0.98, suggesting the presence of numerous structural defects within the sp2 clusters, possibly attributed to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs [24]. Conversely, in CQDs, the intensity of the D band is higher than that of the G band, indicating the dominance of sp3 structures. The ID/IG ratio of CQDs is 1.45, indicating the presence of numerous structural defects within the sp3 clusters [26].

The optical characteristics of P/Cl-CQDs were investigated using UV spectrum. As depicted in Fig. 4(d), the UV–Vis findings unveil a prominent absorption peak of P/Cl-CQDs at 280 nm, ascribed to the π → π* transition occurring within aromatic sp2 domains [36]. The inset in Fig. 4(d) illustrates the state of P/Cl-CQDs. Under natural light, P/Cl-CQDs exhibit a clear and transparent brownish-yellow color (left inset). Under UV light at 254 nm and 365 nm, P/Cl-CQDs appear emerald green (middle inset) and deep blue (right inset), respectively. The fluorescence (FL) spectra of P/Cl-CQDs are shown in Fig. S4, with emission wavelengths in the range of 395–465 nm when the excitation wavelengths are in the range of 300–400 nm. P/Cl-CQDs have a typical excitation-dependent FL pattern, and the migration of their emission wavelengths occurs probably under the influence of the external parameters, such as dopants and solvents. Doping P and Cl not only modulates the structure of P/Cl-CQDs, but also affects the sensitivity of P/Cl-CQDs to the external environment, which in turn affects the optical properties of P/Cl-CQDs [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation conditions and concentration optimization of P/Cl-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Variations in the preparation conditions of P/Cl-CQDs result in diverse CL intensities, and their effects on the system differ upon their incorporation into the luminol system. Therefore, an investigation was undertaken to explore the optimal preparation time and temperature of P/Cl-CQDs to achieve the maximum CL intensity. At a temperature of 180 °C, the reaction time was adjusted to obtain P/Cl-CQDs with varying CL intensity as shown in Fig. S5(a): with prolonged reaction time, the CL intensity of P/Cl-CQDs gradually increased. Upon the reaction time reached 6 h, the obtained P/Cl-CQDs exhibited the optimal CL signal. Further extension of the reaction time resulted in a slight decrease in the CL intensity of P/Cl-CQDs, possibly due to excessive carbonization caused by prolonged reaction time. Therefore, the preparation time of P/Cl-CQDs should be controlled within 6 h.

With the preparation time of P/Cl-CQDs fixed at 6 h, P/Cl-CQDs were prepared at different temperatures to explore the effect of temperature on their CL intensity. The results are shown in Fig. S5(b) reveals that with an increase in the reaction temperature, the CL intensity of P/Cl-CQDs exhibited a corresponding rise. At 180 °C, the CL intensity of the synthesized P/Cl-CQDs peaked, exhibiting the highest intensity. However, when the preparation temperature exceeded 180 °C, the CL intensity of P/Cl-CQDs started to decline, potentially attributable to the elevated temperature during synthesis, which might compromise the structural integrity of P/Cl-CQDs, consequently impacting the CL intensity. Therefore, maintaining the preparation temperature at 180 °C is most suitable.

As a catalyst in the luminol system, the concentration of P/Cl-CQDs has a significant impact on the CL signal. By fixing the concentrations of the solutions in the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system and adjusting the dilution factor of P/Cl-CQDs to obtain the highest response, the results shown in Fig. S5(c) were obtained: increasing the dilution factor of P/Cl-CQDs enhanced the CL intensity of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system. When P/Cl-CQDs were diluted to 600 times, the CL intensity reached its maximum value. Further dilution of P/Cl-CQDs resulted in a decrease in CL intensity. Therefore, P/Cl-CQDs diluted 600 times was selected to constitute the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system for STM and STX determination.

As an oxidant in the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, the concentration of K3Fe(CN)6 significantly affects the CL intensity for the determination of STM and STX [39]. Fig. S6(a) illustrates the detection of STM using different concentrations of K3Fe(CN)6 as the system oxidant. With the escalation of K3Fe(CN)6 concentration, the CL signal of STM progressively intensifies. At the concentration of 4 × 10−4 mol/L for K3Fe(CN)6 reaches, the detection of STM yields the highest CL signal. Further increases in the concentration of K3Fe(CN)6 result in a noticeable decrease in the CL signal. Therefore, for STM detection, the concentration of K3Fe(CN)6 should be optimized at 4 × 10−4 mol/L.

As shown in Fig. S6(d), when detecting STX, the CL intensity gradually increases with the elevation of K3Fe(CN)6 concentration. The highest CL intensity for STX detection is achieved when the concentration of K3Fe(CN)6 reaches 2 × 10−4 mol/L. Beyond this concentration, the CL intensity for STX detection begins to decline. Hence, for the detection of STX, the concentration of K3Fe(CN)6 should be maintained at 2 × 10−4 mol/L.

Luminol reacts with K3Fe(CN)6 in alkaline solution to produce CL intensity, where NaOH provides the alkaline environment for the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, while its concentration plays a pivotal role in influencing the CL intensity. The relationship between NaOH concentration and CL signal intensity was investigated, as shown in Fig. S6(b). With an increase in NaOH concentration, the obtained CL intensity rises accordingly. Upon attaining a concentration of 0.2 M, NaOH yields the maximum CL intensity for STM detection, however, subsequent increments in NaOH concentration precipitate a decline in CL intensity. As such, a NaOH concentration of 0.2 M is deemed optimal for STM detection.

Fig. S6(e) demonstrates the CL response for detecting STX under different NaOH concentrations. A discernible augmentation in the CL signal for STX detection is evident with the elevation of NaOH concentration. An optimal CL response for STX detection is attained upon reaching a NaOH concentration of 0.18 M. Beyond a NaOH concentration of 0.18 M, the CL signal for STX detection begins to weaken. Therefore, opting for a NaOH concentration of 0.18 M is deemed most suitable for the detection of STX.

Luminol, as a CL reagent, has a crucial impact on CL intensity due to its concentration [40]. Under fixed experimental conditions, optimizing the luminol concentration can yield enhanced CL signals for the detection of both STM and STX. As depicted in Fig. S6(c), increasing the concentration of luminol results in a significant enhancement of the CL signal. At a luminol concentration of 1.2 × 10−5 mol/L, the maximum CL signal for STM detection is attained. Further increases in luminol concentration leads to a decline in the CL signal. Thus, the optimal luminol concentration for STM detection is determined to be 1.2 × 10−5 mol/L.

As depicted in Fig. S6(f), within a certain range, higher Luminol concentrations result in higher CL intensities for detecting STX. When the luminol concentration is 1.8 × 10−5 mol/L, the highest CL signal for detecting STX is achieved. Upon surpassing a luminol concentration of 1.8 × 10−5 mol/L, the CL signal exhibits a gradual decrease. To ensure optimal STX detection, the luminol concentration should be consistently maintained at 1.8 × 10−5 mol/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The linear response range and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, a series of standard solutions containing STM and STX were measured. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated according to the IUPAC definition (LOD = 3 SD/K, LOQ = 10 SD/K, where SD is the standard deviation and K is the slope of the linear regression equation). Under the optimal conditions, STM and STX showed linear correlations with CL intensity within the ranges of 1 × 10−6-4 × 10−4 and 5.0 × 10−6-2.8 × 10−4 mol/L, respectively (Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b). The regression equations for determining STM and STX were ΔI = 1.70 × 107 c + 1482.90 (R2 = 0.9991) and ΔI = 2.28 × 107 c + 11962.10 (R2 = 0.9993), respectively. The LOD for STM and STX were 3.44 × 10−7 mol/L and 1.64 × 10−7 mol/L, respectively, while the LOQ were 1.15 × 10−6 mol/L and 5.47 × 10−7 mol/L, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD) for eleven consecutive measurements of 1.5 × 10−4 mol/L STM and 1 × 10−4 mol/L STX standard solutions were 1.29 % and 1.23 %, respectively. These results indicate that the detection method exhibits broad linearity, high sensitivity, and good precision.

The CL kinetic curves are depicted in Fig. 5(c). Under the same experimental conditions, the CL signal of the luminol system is relatively low. However, upon the addition of P/Cl-CQDs, the CL signal of the resulting luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system is significantly enhanced. Furthermore, both 1 × 10−4 mol/L of STM and STX exert varying degrees of inhibition on the CL signal of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system. The higher CL intensity of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system can amplify this inhibitory effect, thereby providing higher feasibility for the detection of STM and STX.

The luminol system is a typical direct CL system, with the emitting species being the excited state of 3-aminophthalic acid (APA*), which is the oxidation product of luminol. When APA* returns to its ground state, energy is released, generating light with a wavelength around 425 nm [41]. To explore the CL mechanism, UV–visible absorption spectra were examined under the same conditions for the luminol system, luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, and the systems after the addition of samples. The results revealed that the UV absorption spectra trends of the luminol system, luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STM system, and luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STX system were roughly similar. Both the luminol system and luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system exhibited distinct absorption peaks at 310 nm and 425 nm, indicating that the addition of P/Cl-CQDs did not alter the chemical properties of the original system. P/Cl-CQDs played a catalytic role in the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, and its addition did not lead to the generation of new emitting species, APA* remained the emitting species. However, when STM and STX participated in the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, the UV absorption spectra of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STM system and luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STX system exhibited shifts. The absorption peak at 310 nm shifted to 320 nm, and the absorption value at 425 nm decreased, indicating that chemical reactions occurred between STM, STX, and the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, resulting in the generation of intermediate products.

To investigate the generated intermediates, the influence of different radical scavengers on the CL intensity of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STM system and luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STX system in the presence of P/Cl-CQDs was studied, as shown in Fig. S7. Ascorbic acid (AA), a classic reducing agent and radical scavenger, is commonly used as a radical scavenger. When the concentration of AA was 1 × 10−3 M, the CL intensity of both the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STM system and luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs)-STX system was significantly suppressed, further confirming that the CL reaction proceeds via a free radical pathway. Thiourea is a common •OH radical scavenger, by adding thiourea (1 × 10−3 M), it was confirmed that •OH also participated in the reaction. Nitrotetrazolium Blue chloride (NBT) is widely used to detect •O2 − radicals, as it can be reduced by •O2 − to form a deep blue formazan. When 0.1 M of NBT was added to the system, the CL intensity significantly decreased, confirming the involvement of •O2 − radicals as intermediates in the reaction. L-tryptophan is a scavenger for 1O2, the observed quenching effect in the presence of 0.1 M of L-tryptophan also confirmed the generation of 1O2 in luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, indicating that 1O2 is involved in the CL reaction [42,43].

Based on these results, a possible luminescence mechanism is proposed: the CL reaction between luminol and K3Fe(CN)6 occurs under alkaline conditions, and P/Cl-CQDs enhance the CL signal of this reaction. During this process, active oxygen reaction intermediates such as •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2 are formed. The enhancing effect of P/Cl-CQDs on the CL of the luminol system is attributed to the formation of oxygen-containing intermediates. •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2intermediates accelerate the oxidation reaction of luminol, thereby enhancing CL emission. On the other hand, •OH and •O2 − generated in the system can react with APA through hole and electron injection processes to produce APA•+ and APA•−, which then undergo electron transfer quenching to generate APA*. Additionally, 1O2 transfers its energy to APA, generating APA* upon returning to the ground state, which emits strong photons. When STM or STX were added to the system, they competitively consume •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2 intermediates, slowing down the oxidation reaction of luminol and reducing the generation of APA•+ and APA•−, leads to a reduction in the number of species emitting from the system and ultimately reducing the CL intensity. The possible CL mechanism is shown in Scheme 1 .

Under optimized conditions, as shown in Fig. S8, we investigated whether the presence of different heavy metal ions affects the CL intensity of the system. Representative interfering substances commonly encountered during the detection process of STM and STX were added as interference agents to examine the effectiveness and stability of the method. Interference study was conducted for 3 × 10−4 mol/L of STM and STX, respectively. The impact of interfering factors such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, BrO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, CH3COO−, HCOO−, Lys, Arg, Gly, soluble starch, glucose, and citric acid on the detection of STM and STX was investigated. The results of interference study are summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis of TFA and STZ
ce:section.ce:para: Weighing 10 g of ground pork samples, 10 mL of ethyl acetate was added and stirred for 5 min. Then, 2 mL of 0.1 M HCl was added to the extraction solution. The mixture was rotary evaporated to about 2 mL, followed by the addition of 1 mL HCl and 2 mL n-hexane. The combined solution was vortexed for 30 s and centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 5 min. The hexane layer was discarded, retaining the lower layer solution. Subsequently, it underwent filtration using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the sample solution for detection. Under optimized experimental conditions, CL measurements were conducted on pork samples prepared in multiple groups with the addition of 0.1 mM standard STM. The results showed that the concentration values of each sample were between 0.99 × 10−4-1.02 × 10−4 mol/L. Additionally, CL measurements were performed on pork samples prepared in each group with the addition of 1 × 10−4 mol/L standard STX. The determined concentration values of each sample ranged from 0.96 × 10−4-1.04 × 10−4 mol/L, indicating no residual STM and STX in the tested pork samples.

To evaluate the accuracy of this method, various concentrations of standard STM were added to each test sample, and the recovery rate of STM was examined. The results are shown in Table 2 : The recovery rate of STM ranged from 100.0 % to 103.8 %, while that of STX ranged from 98.0 % to 102.3 %. This indicates that the established CL method is stable and reliable, and it can successfully be applied to determine the content of STM and STX in actual samples.

Monitoring the content of STM and STX is of significant importance, and various methods for detecting STM and STX have been developed. A comparison between this study and other similar methods reported in the literature is presented in Table 3 . The data indicate that compared to other reported methods, the new method established in this study has a wider linear range as well as lower LOD and LOQ.

This study utilized Epimedium brevicornu Maxim. as a green carbon source and (NH4)2HPO4 and NH4Cl as dopants. P/Cl-CQDs was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method, exhibiting uniform particle distribution, stable properties, good water solubility, and excellent CL performance. P/Cl-CQDs acted as catalysts to enhance the CL intensity of the luminol system. The newly formed luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system exhibited significantly enhanced CL intensity compared to the original system. Both STM and STX could suppress the CL intensity of the luminol-(P/Cl-CQDs) system, enabling the detection of STM and STX. Under optimal conditions, this method could detect STM in the range of 1–400 × 10−6 mol/L, with LOD and RSD values of 0.34 × 10−6 mol/L and 1.29 %, respectively. Similarly, it could detect STX in the range of 5–280 × 10−6 mol/L, with LOD and RSD values of 0.16 × 10−6 mol/L and 1.23 %, respectively. The method successfully determined the content of STM and STX in pork samples, with spike recoveries ranging from 100.0 % to 103.8 % for STM and from 98.0 % to 102.3 % for STX. This detection method is characterized by high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, rapid detection speed, and suitability for practical STM and STX determination in animal-derived food products, thus providing a new approach for detecting residues of other veterinary drugs in animal-sourced foods.

Yue Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yujie Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Xian Yang: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation. Xue Zhang: Supervision, Software, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110893.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, J. M. & Han, S. Q. Strong Enhancement of the Chemiluminescence of Hydrogen Sulfite-Oxidant Systems in the Presence of N,S-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots, and Its Application to the Determination of Folic Acid in Spinach and Kiwifruit Samples. Food Analytical Methods 12, 869-876, doi:10.1007/s12161-018-01419-x (2019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced chemiluminescence of the luminol-K3Fe(CN)6 system by ZnSe quantum dots and its application
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo, C., Zeng, H. J., Ding, X. J. et al. Enhanced chemiluminescence of the luminol-K3Fe(CN)6 system by ZnSe quantum dots and its application. Journal of Luminescence 134, 888-892, doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2012.06.022 (2013).
